title: Proactive Audit

content.question: What system needs a tune-up you've been postponing?
sop: Schedule recurring reviews for your core systems, monthly for business, weekly for personal
Compare what's actually happening against what the system was designed to do
Rate the drift and how long it's gone unchecked (see calculation, page 5)
Make one fix per review, don't try to overhaul everything at once
Log what you found and fixed, so drift is visible over time, not just felt
content.explainer: Systems don't usually fail all at once, they drift, a small deviation at a time, and each individual drift feels too small to bother fixing. The problem is that drift compounds with time even when it doesn't compound in severity, which is why a system left unchecked for a year needs a bigger fix than the same system caught monthly.
questions: What system needs a tune-up you've been postponing?
What process in your business hasn't been reviewed since the day you set it up?
If you audited your top 3 systems today, which one would surprise you most with how far it's drifted?
content.equation_example: Drift: rate 1-10 how far the system has wandered from its design. Time: months since last audit. Drift 6 times Time 8 months equals 48, so Health equals 1 divided by 48, approximately 0.02, very low. Catch it at Time 1 month: Drift 6 times 1 equals 6, so Health is approximately 0.17, much healthier. Higher health score means the system still matches its design; lower means drift has been compounding unchecked for too long.
content.closing_statement: Unchecked drift always costs more the longer it waits.

caption:
Principle Nº 233: Proactive Audit

Regularly inspect and fine-tune systems.

Ask yourself: What system needs a tune-up you've been postponing?
